steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence refers to Descartes’s one famous phrase. The blank must supply a singular possessive noun (“phrase’s”) and a singular pronoun (“its”) that both agree in number with that single phrase.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — “phrases’ simplicity masks its”: phrases’ is a plural possessive, but the passage names only one phrase. Plural possessive on a singular referent violates number agreement on the noun. ✗
  • Choice B — “phrases simplicity masks their”: phrases is a bare plural with no possessive apostrophe, making the noun non-possessive; and their is plural, mismatching the single phrase. Two errors: missing possessive and plural pronoun. ✗
  • Choice C — “phrase’s simplicity masks their”: phrase’s is correctly singular possessive, but their is a plural pronoun that disagrees with the singular noun phrase. Pronoun-antecedent number agreement is violated. ✗
  • Choice D — “phrase’s simplicity masks its”: phrase’s is singular possessive and its is a singular pronoun — both agree in number with the one phrase Descartes coined. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
